item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, and I'm giving it a +1, bit there are a whole bunch of tonal issues, particurally in the addenda. You use 'us' and 'we' quote a bit in there and you should either commit entirely to 3rd person or to 1st person (ie the direct text of the report). This is just an example, and there are quite a few similar issues throughout that really need to e corrected to fix this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have changed the scientific properties of SCP-4094 to be more realistic. I thank you again for your advice. I hope you find that this article is better than it was previously!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this one. The basic concept of nano machines sent by aliens to destroy us has always terrified me, and its adaption into an organic is a mostly surface level yet nice twist. I think there are a few relatively easy changes you can make to really improve it.

First don't make quite so indestructible. This thing is scary because of how fast it replicates, and how so much of is lurking in a thawing lake. While cold temperatures should still be the most effective way to eliminate it (it's a nice motif), having it be destroyed at relatively high temperatures and pressures that are difficult to produce is enough. Perhaps make it highly resistant to radiation, which is reasonable, and makes it so that on site nukes will not be enough to contain it. Which seems to be the Foundation's favorite fallback measure.

Second the translation feels really off. Either its an alien language we have no clue about, or the Foundation bites the bullet and uses anomalous means to translate the message. If the former no point really in even having a log. If the latter it should come out with a coherent message, even if words are lacking for a direct synonym. They are usually describable.

Also what actions is the Foundation taking to secure the lake? Honestly, testing this on a new site built on the lake seems reasonable given the threat.

All that said I really enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that if you said translated through anomalous means, you could get away with explaining the translation without an train of articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello, and thank you for your critique.

I will admit that the page needs to be reworked which I will likely do in the coming weeks (I'd also like to add an image as well when that happens). The science here is definitely softer than is the standard for the Foundation articles which will be changed soon.

As for the destruction goals, I tried to add in the addenda that destruction was not just one aim of the Foundation regarding it, and they are attempting to adapt it. At the same time, I wanted to emphasize how destructive it was to a point where the Foundation is seeking to eliminate or mitigate it in some way so as to prevent the implied effects of it. I was likely too subtle with it.

You've given me quite a bit to reflect upon, and I will be amending the points you have made in the article within the next month or so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm. This seems to be suffering from the simple case of "too much bad science".
I haven't read it all, I admit, but I'm only here to comment on what irritates me immeasurably while reading this.

+ expand
- close

PH of 1.3

1.3? Why not use 1?

Molten tungsten

Why not just put it in a blast furnace or something? Why do you need to go to the bother of melting all that tungsten that will no doubt cool immediately.

100G of atmospheric pressure

Nah. It would be fluid pressure (water). Nobody would go to the bother of using air (or atmosphere).

And it can survive 100G, but gets broken under a hydraulic press? What??

7500C degrees won't kill it? That's hot enough to form plasma out of iron, and hotter than the surface of the sun! How much energy would that require? What on Earth (quite literally) would you make the walls for this insaaaane crucible out of?

Also, why are the Foundation so hellbent on expending so many resources to destroy this thing? Its not exactly part of their ethos, either.

I'm aware this is insanely nitpick-y, but there are far more cases than this, and I find it unbearable to read. It just feels like someone has gone to google and searched up, I don't know, what's the metal with the highest melting point (tungsten?). I'm also getting massive vibes of 682-esque extreme destruction measures. Either way, just remember using scientific terminology to be overly-specific wastes time and is only effective if 1) reasonably spaced and 2) true.

Cool science alone isn't enough to sustain an article. I learnt this the hard way myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, thank you for your critique. As I posted above, I was not expecting a knockout blow on my first SCP and I will admit this one is flawed in several distinct ways, but I will answer your questions as best I can.

The message is instructions yes; initially I wanted to have a signal receiver that would take the message and convert it into something simpler but I thought that keeping it simple would be better for this article.

As for why the message appears broken, there's a lot I could offer to answer that.

  • No language is 100% translatable to another one.
  • The Foundation's efforts are just the first step; translating some ancient texts takes years and sometimes requires additional eyes on it over time to develop a more coherent translation.
  • It is akin to military short-hand communication, either due to the need for rapid communication or the resources required to transmit a message across huge distances in space (this was my clearest intention with it)

I was additionally hoping to explore how the Foundation decoded these signals, perhaps through another SCP article or a tale - the initial plan was to have several SCP articles that fed into each other but I've put that on ice for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the "message" instructions for the organic nano-machines? How did we translate the signal? Why are so many words in it similar to English words but a couple of characters off? Is it translated or not? How can you "decode" completely alien language or an organic machine's programming language and get broken English?

item.title: Re: First Attempt at a Finished SCP
item.content:encoded: I was hoping to keep it in the range of a believable size for a microbe or a bacteria, but that is a flaw with this one for sure. I'm hoping to tie it into another skip that I'm writing that could shed more light on the thought process behind it if it can be done.

item.title: Re: First Attempt at a Finished SCP
item.content:encoded: Its a good idea, but it needs to be bigger than a micro-meter. At that size level, you will not be able to "move" it anywhere.

item.title: First Attempt at a Finished SCP
item.content:encoded: I had written this several months back and received good quality feedback on it. After a few months where I tightened up the language and cut back a significant part, I have finally put it out. Not expecting a knockout on a first skip, but I hope its an adequate showing.
